My visual data story

Stats 220 Project 4 – Fencing before and after

Introduction

For my project 2, I observed the number of points I won vs lost in bouts of foil fencing on different days. Now, a few months later, we can look at the data and see what’s changed. Have I got better? Worse?

Some of the things I looked at last time were: - Average length of bouts on different days - Win ratio of bouts on different days - My confidence going into a bout.

Adding ranking to the data

In order to judge the skill level of the crowd each day, I looked at my confidence going into the bout, as well as the win ratio for the bout. After project 2 finished, I realised that there’s a better option: the actual ranking of my opponent! Here’s a plot showing the average ranking of my opponents each day:

A plot showing the rankings of opponents by day

I also graphed my performance against ranking:

As you can see there’s a clear (and expected) trend: higher ranked opponents (with small numerical ranking) result in worse win ratios.

(I actually love this plot because I can look at the outliers and remember exactly what caused them.)

The other cool thing I can do with the ranking statistic is look at what rankings I fence the most. I made a density plot to show this:

Finally, I coded something to see which fencers I’ve fenced the most often. (Using ranking as a sort of primary key since during this project everybody had a different ranking, though this isn’t suitable in the long term). During this project the person I fenced the most had rank 79, and this person is a friend of mine so it makes sense.

Number of points per day

Based on Project 4, I decided that Fridays didn’t have nearly enough fencing opportunities, so I decided to switch my efforts to Tuesdays and Thursdays. Here’s a graph showing the effect:

Did I get better?

That’s the million dollar question: did I improve between the two projects. It’s a little hard to say definitively because I don’t have as much data for project 4 as I did for project 2, but here’s the graph:

As you can see, my average win ratio increased during Tuesday and Thursday, which are the most consistent days in terms of opponent skill level. You can also see another reason that I stopped going on Fridays: the win ratio was too high, and I felt like it would be more productive to find more challenging opponents.

So that’s that! I look forward to throwing statistics at more of my hobbies in future.

I, left, score a point against my friend from AUT
